title: Wood stove installation: what the process involves
subtitle: HETAS certification, chimney requirements, and what you can and cannot do yourself
excerpt: Installing a wood stove requires a HETAS-certified installer for the connection, a chimney liner sized to the stove's flue collar, and a non-combustible hearth of the correct dimensions. In smoke control zones, only DEFRA-exempt stoves are permitted. A typical installation costs depending on the chimney condition.
sourceNotes: HETAS (Heating Equipment Testing and Approval Scheme) registered installers guidance. DEFRA smoke control area exempted appliances register (DEFRA: approved stoves for smoke control areas). Building Regulations Approved Document J (combustion appliances). HETAS H003 technical guide.

content.text: Installing a wood stove is notifiable work under Part J of the Building Regulations. You can use a HETAS-registered installer or apply to the local authority before work starts. With the HETAS route, the installer self-certifies and issues a completion certificate.

Most UK urban and suburban areas are smoke control zones. In a smoke control zone, only approved stoves may be operated: stoves tested and approved to meet emission limits for particulates and carbon monoxide. Check whether your area is a smoke control zone via the government or local council website before choosing a stove. In rural areas outside smoke control zones, any stove is permitted.
Minimum height: 4.5 m effective height from appliance draught diverter to top of flue terminal.

Liner diameter: sized to the stove's flue collar. A 5 kW stove typically requires a 150 mm (6-inch) liner; 8-12 kW stoves often require 200 mm. The stove manufacturer's installation manual specifies the minimum flue size.

Existing chimney: most Victorian and Edwardian chimneys can be lined with a flexible stainless steel liner, which is inserted from above and connected to the stove. A HETAS installer will carry out a visual inspection and smoke test before lining.
Twin-wall insulated flue: if there is no existing chimney, a twin-wall flue can go internally or externally. Internal gives better draught. External is more common in retrofits.
Constructional hearth: at least 125 mm thick, 840 mm deep (from the front of the firebox opening), and 840 mm wide. Must be non-combustible (concrete, natural stone, ceramic tile).
Superimposed hearth: a decorative layer on top of the constructional hearth. Must be non-combustible. Laminate, vinyl, and engineered wood are not permitted.

A wood stove connected to an existing chimney does not require planning permission in England (permitted development). An external twin-wall flue is also usually permitted development. Listed buildings require listed building consent for any new flue or alteration to the existing chimney. Conservation areas may have additional conditions on flue visibility.
